mainText:
**Come Sail Away With Me (in Sheboygan, Wisconsin)**
April 11, 2026

“Sheboygan. Very big in Sheboygan,” reminisced John Candy’s polka-playing Gus Polinski in *Home Alone*. Six hundred and twenty-three fictional albums sold in a city that's fluctuated between either side of 50,000 in population since 1970—not bad for an evening of German accordion music at the Elks Club.

Like New York state’s Schenectady—where some of Sheboygan’s earliest settlers hailed from—Sheboygan is a delightful name for a waterside city that never remains submerged for long before being rediscovered by pop culture. New acquaintances chuckle upon hearing a Sheboyganite’s place of origin, swishing its elements like sommeliers, before turning to a friend and hollering, “Hey! Joe! This guy’s from Shah-BOY-gihn!”

The local running joke suggests the name is derived from an old Indian story where the wife of a Menominee chief had another son: “She-had-a-boy-again.” The truth speaks to anglicized Menominee; "Sheboygan" is the result of Yankee and German dialects attempting to convey Sāpīwaehekaneh—“at a hearing distance in the woods” or “great noise underground,” likely referring to the area's waterfalls.

Sheboygan, with its gorgeous beaches and vibrant blue waters during summer (earning it the title “Malibu of the Midwest”) contrasts sharply with its tempestuous gray depths in winter. My attachment to Sheboygan solidified during my teen years, when classmates aimed to escape. Though I spent time in Charleston and Coronado—both undeniably beautiful—I was grateful for the brief days I spent back home, which had more vibrancy and warmth than the months elsewhere.

My wife, a Texan who summered in Sheboygan, correctly observed that everyone can be the main character here. With her resolve to marry a Sheboygan boy, she achieved that goal when we officially wed in 2016 at Fountain Park Methodist Church.

Sheboygan often epitomizes a Reaganite three-legged stool: churches, cheese, and chairs, with bars silently factoring into this mix. Almost every street corner boasts a church; if there's no steeple in sight, it generally implies that the tavern league beat the congregants to the prime real estate, or perhaps parishioners sought to keep their post-Communion activities a secret.

Among America’s most pickled cities, Sheboygan is known for much-cherished establishments like 3 Sheeps Brewing, sun-faded Pabst Blue Ribbon signs, and, for tourists, Wisconsin-exclusive Spotted Cow beer on tap.
